summary: County Wexford is where my great-great-great-grandfather John Francis Coad Sr was born in 1842. It's also the place where the surname Codd, a variant of Coad, is very common, which helped me trace my family's origins. This connection to County Wexford is a key part of understanding where my ancestors came from before they immigrated to the United States.

My ancestors immigrated to the United States around the mid-19th century, with John Francis Coad Sr arriving in 1850 or possibly 1856. The U.S. became the place where our family established themselves, especially in places like Albany, New York, and Nebraska. It was here that they contributed to industries like cattle ranching and helped build communities and institutions.

Albany, New York was the first American city where my great-great-great-grandfather John Francis Coad Sr and his family settled after arriving from Ireland. According to his 1910 obituary, the family lived there before moving westward. Albany represents the initial foothold my family had in the United States during the 1850s.

Nebraska is where my family really made their mark, especially in the cattle industry in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. My ancestors helped start the cattle business in western Nebraska and were involved in civic life and education there. This state became home to many of the Coad family's contributions and legacies.

Omaha, Nebraska is my hometown and the place where my mother's side of the family, including the Coads, played a significant role in banking, civic life, and education. The Omaha World-Herald published my great-great-great-grandfather John Francis Coad Sr's obituary in 1910, highlighting his importance there. Omaha represents the heart of my family's history and community involvement.

John Francis Coad Sr is my great-great-great-grandfather, born in County Wexford, Ireland, in 1842. He immigrated to the U.S. around 1850 with his parents and settled first in Albany, New York before moving west. He was involved in the cattle industry in Nebraska and left a legacy documented in his 1910 obituary and the 1938 volume of Nebraska History.

Patrick Coad was my great-great-great-great-grandfather and the father of John Francis Coad Sr. He immigrated from Ireland, possibly arriving in Baltimore, Maryland in 1850 under the surname variant 'Codd.' He was illiterate, which might explain the name change, and he may have come to the U.S. ahead of his family, with his wife and children following later.

Anna Kelly Coad was my great-great-great-great-grandmother and the wife of Patrick Coad. She emigrated from County Wexford, Ireland, and settled in the United States with her family. Though details about her arrival are less clear, she played a part in the family's move to Albany, New York, and eventually Nebraska.

Boston was a likely port of entry for my ancestors when they immigrated to the United States in 1850. An article about my great-great-great-grandfather John Francis Coad Sr's cattle industry work, found in Nebraska History, suggested Boston as their arrival point. Although I initially searched passenger records there without success, it was an important location in my research.

Baltimore, Maryland is where I found a passenger record for 'P. Codd' that matched my ancestor Patrick Coad's details, suggesting he may have arrived there in 1850. This discovery was key because it indicated that Patrick might have immigrated separately from the rest of the family. Baltimore thus represents an important clue in piecing together my family's immigration story.

Nebraska History is a publication where I found a 1938 article about my great-great-great-grandfather John Francis Coad Sr's involvement in the cattle industry. This volume helped me identify Boston as a possible port of entry for my ancestors. Nebraska History has been a valuable resource in understanding my family's contributions to the state's development.

My family helped found a major Jesuit university in Nebraska, which is part of the legacy left by my ancestors. This involvement in education highlights the broader impact the Coad family had beyond business and politics. The Jesuit university stands as a testament to their commitment to community and learning.
